An evaluation rubric will be provided to each dissertation committee member who will grade, on a scale of one to five, whether the student: conducted appropriate research according to the goals and objectives of the research design; achieved a research outcome and primary argument demonstrating an important intervention or contribution to current debate(s) in the discipline; demonstrated an ability to productively engage with intellectual challenges and critiques provided in the question and answer period; utilized analytic abilities and articulated conceptual and methodological material in verbal form.
results_conclusions: Across the five dissertations and defenses evaluated by faculty committee members, 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 2.9; 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 3.9; 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 4.4; 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 4.5, and 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 4.8. This results in 80% of students achieving the expected threshold of a 3.5 average with 20% failing to achieve that goal.
improvement_plan: Our results show that most students completing their dissertation this year excelled in achieving the programâ€™s stated expectations. However, one studentâ€™s performance did not fulfill the programâ€™s expectations for pedagogical achievement. It is recommended that, in the future, students who are at risk of not fulfilling the programâ€™s expectations be counseled closely by the chair of their dissertation committee (and other committee members when appropriate) to focus attention on the areas of evaluation to be assessed (per PLO 3 above). The impact of these changes will be assessed in AY 24-25.

plo: Identify and define relevant research topics in Electrical and Computer Engineering and conduct independent research with results that advance the state of the art in the field.
methods: The ECE student in the MS-PhD program must successfully complete a project course ELEC 599 in their second semester (or first semester if they matriculate in Spring).  Students who have already acquired a masterâ€™s degree elsewhere must also complete the ELEC 599 project, after which acceptance of their previous masterâ€™s degree will be determined by the Graduate Committee. ELEC 599 allows students to begin research early in the Ph.D. program and also crucially serves as the vehicle to identify the PhD thesis advisor. ELEC 599 consists of two parts: research and communications seminars. At the end of the semester, students are evaluated through written project reports and oral presentations by at least three faculty members, including their potential advisors. Students must pass ELEC 599 with a grade of A- or better to remain in the PhD program. Materials and methods of assessment: A total of 30 students who matriculated in Fall 2023 and Spring 2024 took ELEC 599 and were evaluated for their performance. The ability to identify and define relevant research topics was evaluated by studentsâ€™ problem statements and research objectives. The clearness and conciseness of the problem statement were ranked on a scale of 1-5, with 5 (Excellent) being the highest performance. A score of 4 (Very Good) means the problem statement is at an appropriate level but may not be clear or concise. 3 (Good) was given when the problem is not stated clearly or with sufficient detail. Below 3 would be deemed inadequate in problem statement skills.
results_conclusions: Results: All 30 students scored 3 (Good) or above in this evaluation category, which indicates that students are making excellent progress in identifying the problem and defining the research topics. 87% of the students scored above 4 (Very Good) and 30% of the students scored 5 (Excellent). In ELEC 599, students were given opportunities to learn how to craft problem statements, hypotheses, and experimental objectives through multiple lectures, peer reviews, and class activities. The assessment suggests that the course curriculum was effective in helping students achieve PLO1 in the early stage of their program.
improvement_plan: ELEC 599 is required for all students in their first year, and the tool is useful as a formative assessment of PLO1. No specific changes to the course are suggested at this time. We will continue to reinforce this training through various methods in ELEC 599 and assess the learning outcome annually. We will also try to employ methods such as evaluation of MS thesis defense, PhD qualifier, PhD proposal, and PhD thesis defense. These may have to be sample data as students go through different stages of exams/evaluations in different timelines. In general, we try to monitor studentsâ€™ progress and ensure that milestones are met by the expected timeline with an expected outcome. We will increase communications with students and faculty advisors so they will have a clear understanding of the requirements and will not fall behind. The graduate committee will evaluate the effectiveness of this assessment plan on a regular basis and revise it as needed.

plo: Solve real-world problems by integrating knowledge gained in courses and through independent study.
methods: We used the evaluations from the Annual Progress Reports completed by both students and their advisors and the graduation rate to assess this Program Learning Outcome. Materials and methods of assessment: The ability to solve real-world problems by integrating knowledge gained in courses and through independent study (PLO3) was evaluated by the studentâ€™s overall research performance through Annual Progress Reports. The Annual Progress Report is completed annually by both students and their advisors. Students provide their course and research progress, publications toward PhD, milestones achieved, and plans for the degree. The advisors evaluated their studentsâ€™ research progress on four scales: a) exceed expectations, b) meet expectations, c) borderline, and d) do not meet expectations. The expectation to satisfy PLO3 is to reach 80% of all students â€śmeet or exceed expectationsâ€ť and 30% of all students â€śexceed expectations.â€ť We also looked at the graduation rate within a certain period of time. In ECE, students are expected to defend their thesis by the end of the 11th semester and graduate by the end of the 12th semester. The defense deadline of the department is the end of the 14th semester. We looked at the number of students who would graduate by August 2024 among the number of qualified students (those who obtained MS at Rice or came with an approved prior Masterâ€™s). The expectation to satisfy PLO3 is to reach 80% of qualified students to complete the degree by the end of their 14th semester and 60% of students to complete the degree by the end of their 12th semester.
results_conclusions: Results: The Annual Progress Reports from AY23-24 were conducted during the summer and were collected for 129 PhD students. 87% of the students were evaluated as â€śmeet or exceed expectations,â€ť and 36% were considered â€śexceed expectationsâ€ť by their advisors. 4 students were evaluated as â€śdoes not meet expectations,â€ť and one of them left the program. The result indicates that most students are making good progress towards the degree and expecting to be successful in solving real-world problems by integrating knowledge gained in courses and through their research. As for the graduation rate, out of 13 students who matriculated in Fall 2017 and passed beyond the PhD qualifier (either obtained MS at Rice or came with approved prior Masterâ€™s), 12 students (92%) have graduated by this academic year. For those who matriculated in Fall 2018, 13 students have graduated out of 21 qualified students (62%). The graduation rate is also important in evaluating studentsâ€™ overall progress, not only in research. The result indicates that the department is on track and meeting expectations for the PLO3.
improvement_plan: As the program grows in size and the areas of research become diverse inviting more faculty from outside of the department to serve as advisors, one of the challenges we face is to closely monitor studentsâ€™ progress and keep them on track to meet the expected timeline. We will increase communications with students and faculty advisors so they will have a clear understanding of the requirements and will not fall behind. We will also try to employ methods such as evaluation of MS thesis defense, PhD qualifier, PhD proposal, and PhD thesis defense. We will look at the overall evaluation but also use a particular rubric such as â€śprospects for success,â€ť which looks at studentsâ€™ ability to share new insights with others, knowledge of related publications and industry standards, and ability to demonstrate an understanding of theoretical and systems methodologies. These may have to be sample data as students go through different stages of exams/evaluations in different timelines. The graduate committee will evaluate the effectiveness of this assessment plan on a regular basis and revise it as needed.

plo: Apply sociological knowledge and training to understand theory and policy oriented around issues of human well-being in the US and globally, including how to understand the relationship between inequality and factors like race, class, gender, and education.
methods: Instructors of SOCI 301 (Social Inequality), SOCI 381 (Research Methods), and other substantive 300-level courses were asked to complete standardized rubrics to assess studentsâ€™ understanding and application of key social issues that reveal the relationship between social and economic characteristics and inequality in a variety of outcomes, ranging from health to educational attainment. Instructors of each of the 7 relevant courses offered in AY 23-24 evaluated a representative sample of students from their class on a 10-item rubric using a 0â€“3-point scale, with 0 indicating â€śstudent does not demonstrate knowledge or skillâ€ť and 3 indicating â€śstudent has mastered this knowledge or skill.â€ť
results_conclusions: The department has set a goal of 80% of students scoring an average of 2.0 or better on rubric total score out of a possible 3 points. Every student evaluated in the relevant courses exceeded this goal, with average student-specific scores across all rubric items ranging from 2.125 to 3. Across all relevant courses, the average overall score on the 10-item rubric was 2.67 (class minimum: 2.55; class maximum: 2.875). The cross-class average scores for each of the 10 rubric items ranged from 2.35 to 2.85.
improvement_plan: Given the strong performance of students on the assessment rubrics, we are very pleased with current performance on PLO 3 and plan to continue our current approach in these classes. We will reevaluate student performance in AY 24-25 with the same assessment rubric to ensure that we are maintaining our high standards for producing students who are well able to apply sociological insights to real world issues.

department: Kinesiology Department/Sports Medicine & Exercise Physiology Program, BA
plo: Apply theoretical and practical knowledge of psychological factors in sport, exercise, rehabilitation, and performance settings.
methods: Three exams are administered in KINE 310 â€“ Psychological Aspects of Sport and Exercise to assess student understanding of course content.   Topics for exam 1 include motivation, foundations of sport psychology, leadership and communication in sport, and self-confidence.  Topics for exam 2 include understanding anxiety stress and mood in sport and exercise, attention and concentration in sport, and coping and intervention strategies for sport and exercise including imagery, goal setting and self-talk. In addition to the 2 examinations, the students are assessed by conducting case studies and behavior change projects. The topics are all related to Psychological issues in Sports.
results_conclusions: The students showed learning and retention of knowledge regarding Psychological Aspects of Sports, which includes topics related to performance anxiety, attention, concentration, motivation, etc.â€¦ The average score for the examinations and the case studies and behavioral change exercises was over 85%. Seventeen out of 20 students (85%) (17/20) scored above 85%. The department is satisfied with these results.
improvement_plan: While currently no major changes are planned, our evaluation and assessment tools/rubrics are annually reviewed during the subsequent academic year to polish and improve to ensure that these documents fairly and adequately assess the studentsâ€™ project and this Program Learning Outcome.

plo: Lead research and design groups by communicating innovative ideas effectively.
methods: The ECE student in the MS-PhD program must successfully complete a project course ELEC 599 in their second semester (or first semester if they matriculate in Spring).  Students who have already acquired a masterâ€™s degree elsewhere must also complete the ELEC 599 project, after which acceptance of their previous masterâ€™s degree will be determined by the Graduate Committee. ELEC 599 allows students to begin research early in the Ph.D. program and also crucially serves as the vehicle to identify the PhD thesis advisor. ELEC 599 consists of two parts: research and communications seminars. At the end of the semester, students are evaluated through written project reports and oral presentations by at least three faculty members, including their potential advisors. Students must pass ELEC 599 with a grade of A- or better to remain in the PhD program Materials and methods of assessment: A total of 30 students who matriculated in Fall 2023 and Spring 2024 took ELEC 599 and were evaluated for their performance. The ability to lead research by communicating innovative ideas effectively was evaluated by studentsâ€™ written and oral presentation skills and the presentation of data. The written presentation skills were evaluated from several viewpoints, such as appealing design, good writing style, easy reading of the report, appropriate citation, and grammatical errors. The oral presentation skills were evaluated by presenting manners such as eye contact, use of fillers, and level of confidence. The presentation of data was evaluated by the design and use of graphics, whether it is informative and easy to interpret. Those skills were evaluated separately on a scale of 1-5, with 5 (Excellent) being the highest performance, and we pulled the average scores. Scores above 3 (Good) are considered passing scores with opportunity for, but without pressing need for improvement.  Below 3 would be deemed inadequate in communication skills.
results_conclusions: Results: Out of 30 students evaluated, no student scored below 3 (Good). 83% scored above 4 (Very Good) and 17% of the students scored 5 (Excellent).  In general, students are making good progress in communication skills. The goal is to bring 20% of students to score 5. Several lectures, peer reviews, and class activities were conducted to improve studentsâ€™ writing and oral presentation skills. Midterm evaluations were also conducted to assess their oral presentation skills. The assessment suggests that the course curriculum was overall effective in helping students achieve PLO2, but there is room for some improvement.
improvement_plan: Coaching sessions and mentorship by senior ECE students and/or trained student coaches coordinated by the School of Engineering can be implemented as additional training for reinforcing communication skills. We will continue to provide various training to build their communication skills in ELEC 599 and assess the learning outcome annually. We will also try to employ methods such as evaluation of MS thesis defense, PhD qualifier, PhD proposal, and PhD thesis defense. These may have to be sample data as students go through different stages of exams/evaluations in different timelines. In general, we try to monitor studentsâ€™ progress and ensure that milestones are met by the expected timeline with an expected outcome. We will increase communications with students and faculty advisors so they will have a clear understanding of the requirements and will not fall behind. The graduate committee will evaluate the effectiveness of this assessment plan on a regular basis and revise it as needed.

plo: Conducted responsible, ethically appropriate research with their interlocutors and consultants in a world of increasingly complex interplay between small-scale and large-scale concerns and commitments. Conduct responsible, ethical research with interlocutors and consultants in a world of increasingly complex interplay between small-scale and large-scale concerns and commitments.
methods: Each student will be evaluated directly by the faculty on the dissertation committee, who will base their evaluation on both (a) the quality of the written material (dissertation) and (b) the oral presentation of research materials (dissertation defense). An evaluation rubric will be provided to each dissertation committee member who will grade, on a scale of one to five, whether the student: conducted ethically appropriate research; utilized a research design that demonstrated an awareness of small-scale dynamics within the research site and among interlocutors; and developed a research design demonstrating an awareness of large-scale dynamics within the research site and among interlocutors, pertinent to the research objective.
results_conclusions: Across the five dissertations and defenses evaluated by faculty committee members, 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 2.9; 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 4.1, 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 4.2, 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 4.4 and 20% (1 student) achieved an average of 5. This results in 80% of students achieving the expected threshold of a 3.5 average, and scoring well above the minimum threshold.
improvement_plan: Based on our results, we do not anticipate making changes to the expectations of PLO 5 or training toward those goals. It is recommended that, in the future, students who are at risk of not fulfilling the programâ€™s expectations be counseled closely by the chair of their dissertation committee (and other committee members when appropriate) to focus attention on the areas of evaluation to be assessed (per PLO 5 above).

plo: Apply advanced knowledge from several major areas of modern chemical engineering.
methods: The two main sources for assessment are (1) projects in our graduate core courses and (2) the thesis proposal. For 2023/24, we examined student performance on the most recent round of thesis proposals, presented in June 2023.  Students write and defend their thesis proposal before their thesis committee at the end of their second year of residency.  This proposal is expected to represent an in-depth examination by the student of the state of knowledge in their chosen research topic, as well as an extensive plan for their research.  In length, this proposal is typically 50% of the eventual full PhD thesis. Students are judged on several criteria, including: (1) Research Objectives, (2) Literature Review, (3) Preliminary Results and (4) Proposed Research.  In addition to the overall Pass/Fail grading, the written proposal and oral presentation are graded on a scale of 1-5 in these categories. Grades of 3-5 are considered satisfactory to excellent. We collected both the thesis proposals and detailed committee grades with comments/feedback.
results_conclusions: For the 15 PhD students starting in 2021 and expected to present their thesis proposals at the end of the 2022-23 academic year, 12 received a passing grade, 1 received a grade of â€śpass with reservationsâ€ť (for which changes and/or additions were requested) and 2 students had to defer their presentations. With respect to each of the above criteria, below are the percentages of students judged to be unsatisfactory (<3), satisfactory (3-4.5) and excellent (>4.5). Students receiving an unsatisfactory grade in one of these criteria were only given a pass with reservation and required to make significant revisions before passing. This was successfully completed in the case of the single student given a pass with reservation.
improvement_plan: We note with concern the deferral of the thesis proposal and defense by two students in this group. This has not happened in many years. These deferrals were partly due to health concerns. In response to this, we monitored the progress 2022 entering class more closely than usual and we are pleased that all of these students are so far on track to defend their proposals by early July of 2024. We are reviewing our current policies and guidance to avoid further deferrals in coming years.

department: Kinesiology Department/Sports Medicine & Exercise Physiology Program, BA
plo: Translate fundamental principles of human nutrition to practical application in exercise and sport.
methods: In HEAL 103: Nutrition, students are required to complete a Concept Application Assignment in which they will apply multiple concepts of human nutrition to plan a diet for patients with differing health needs.  The assignments were directly assessed utilizing a rubric/rating scale by the teaching faculty, Roberta Anding.  In the Spring semester of the 2023-2024 academic year, there were 41 students enrolled in this course. The rubric/rating scale utilized directly assessed the concept retention out of a scale of 70 possible points.  Several factors went into point totals, including use of scientific references, strength of nutritional plan, justification for planning, assessment of specific patient nutritional needs as it applies to exercise and sport, and other principles of nutrition learned during the semester.
results_conclusions: Application and learning of multiple concepts of human nutrition are directly assessed for students in HEAL 103: Nutrition.  The students showed improvement and learning of the concepts with application theory.  The average score for the projects was 60 out of 70 (85%) possible points, utilizing the rubric as described above. Fifty-one students out of 57 (89%) obtained 85% or more. The department is satisfied with these results.
improvement_plan: While currently no major changes are planned, our evaluation and assessment tools/rubrics are annually reviewed during the subsequent academic year to polish and improve to ensure that these documents fairly and adequately assess the studentsâ€™ project and this Program Learning Outcome.

plo: Execute the steps of the entrepreneurial process.
methods:
Undergraduate students working toward the Entrepreneurship Minor take BUSI 369, New Enterprises, as one of four required core courses. New Enterprises introduces the frameworks and tools to execute the steps of the entrepreneurial process, including evaluating opportunities for a new innovation-based enterprise, conceptualizing and developing a venture plan through an iterative process, articulating venture assumptions, and testing venture assumptions through experimentation. 
To assist the students in learning the steps of the entrepreneurial process through experience and practice, the professor assigns the following two projects: Primary Market Research Presentation â€“ A 7-minute team presentation offering a comprehensive overview of the journey in understanding customer needs and informing strategic decisions to address identified problems in the selected market segment. Go/No-Go presentation â€“ An 8-minute presentation providing a concise evaluation of the venture opportunity, including an elevator pitch overview of the problem, customer, and solution, discussion of encountered hurdles, presentation of remaining obstacles, decision to proceed or halt, and formulation of next steps. The professor uses a rubric for each project to evaluate the quality of a team's presentation related to the steps of the process. The scores are measured on a 0-100% scale. We expect 80% of the students to meet a minimum acceptable average score of 70% on each project.
results_conclusions: Studentsâ€™ performance on their BUSI 369 New Enterprises presentation projects was satisfactory: 97% of the students scored an average of 89.6% on the Primary Market Research Presentation 93% of the students scored an average of 90.85% on the Go/No-Go Presentation. We have exceeded the target of 80% of the students meeting a minimum of 70% average score for each project.
improvement_plan: We were satisfied with the studentsâ€™ performance on these integrative presentations and thus feel assured that they have the requisite understanding to execute the steps of the entrepreneurial process. We may consider adjusting the target for achievement in the next assessment to increase the desired performance target and test it out next year.

plo: Carry out independent research in economics and finance, using and developing mathematical, statistical, econometric, and computational tools
methods: Direct assessment: All our doctoral students are required to take mathematics and statistics camps in the summer prior to starting their doctoral program. The other components of the measure are final grades for all first-year core curriculum (microeconomic theory, macroeconomics and finance, econometrics) and the final course grade for Econ 504 (computational economics). Students with low grades are required to take a make-up qualifying exam.
results_conclusions: The majority of the cohort (Matriculation Year 2024) did well. All but one students attained grades that are sufficient to exempt them from taking qualifying exams.
improvement_plan: We will allow 1st yr PhD students to complement our core curriculum with a graduate course in statistics, or mathematics, upon the approval of the Director of Graduate Studies. The goal is to broaden studentsâ€™ general knowledge in these broadly related fields, in the hopes that they learn useful concepts and tools that will be useful for their dissertation.

plo: Students will be introduced to the law and legal concepts and develop an understanding of the law as it applies to the sports industry in relationship to the legal sector, as well as the broader relationship between the sports industry, law and society.
methods: Assessments to measure outcomes are: Test 1: to assess students understanding of the meaning of â€ślawâ€ť, how law impacts sport; basic legal reasoning, analysis and substantive areas of law relevant to sport covered in the first half of class. Mostly scenario-based fact patterns requiring analysis to mimic real life situations as much as possible. To assess their ability to respond as sports managers. Exam based scoring Rubric is used Test 2: to assess students development of legal reasoning skills, analysis and substantive areas of law related to sport covered in the second half of class. Mostly scenario-based fact patterns requiring analysis to mimic real life situations as much as possible. To assess their ability to respond as sports managers. Exam based scoring Rubric is used Final Legal Research Presentation: to assess students ability to research a sport law related topic and present findings, legal analysis and legal argument on the topic. An oral presentation rubric is used. Final Legal Research Project/Paper: to assess students legal research abilities, legal argument and ability to respond to questions raised regarding their research and topic. A research rubric is used. Use of scenarios and fact patterns to develop analytical thinking; approached used by ABA approved law schools. Test 1 / Weekly Discussion Questions Questions are taken directly from the course materials. A grading key is used to ensure the grading is consistent and points appropriately allocated.  Scenarios are covered throughout the course and students are coached throughout the course on this approach.
results_conclusions: Students tend to struggle a bit on Test 1 due to the 10 questions and 60 minute time constraint. They also are not used to tests that require analysis vs â€śtheâ€ť correct answer. Seems to be a major culture shift for students. Students tended to do better on Test 2 in general, although some students still struggled some with the analytical scenarios. Analysis and critical thinking are import skills for students since this is more closely related to real-life.
improvement_plan: Update the Rubrics based on any changes to the law or added/removed content from the class. This is reviewed each semester to ensure the content and grading remains current. Changing Test 1 from 10 questions (60 minutes timed) to 5 questions (60 minutes timed) Spend even more time on analytical thinking, scenario-based learning and addressing skills associated with this. Each semester assess impact of the changes and continue to adjust as warranted.

plo: Conduct a focused review of the literature and develop a research design to carry out independent research.
methods: Direct assessment: Each second-year student completed a literature survey and developed their research agenda under supervision in our Econ 598 â€“ Research Methods course. ECON 598 â€“ Research Methods Prepares second-year Ph.D. students to conduct quantitative research. After aÂ critical review ofÂ existingÂ economic models, statistical analysis of data and economic evaluations, students develop their own research agenda.
results_conclusions: Two of the students earned a grade of A+, five earned a grade of A and two earned a grade of A-.
improvement_plan: We are satisfied with these results and do not plan to make any changes.

plo: Develop an advanced proficiency in legal analysis, legal reasoning, research and understanding of current and foundational issues in sport by evaluating and communicating the theories, prevailing issues and ethical dilemmas impacting the sports industry. (Specific to Sport Law concentration)
methods: Assessments used are: Test 1 (SMGT 464, SMGT 365). Exam scoring rubrics are used. Weekly Discussion Questions (SMGT 464, SMGT 465, SMGT 365). Scoring rubrics are used. Scored Sports Law Topic Debates (6) (SMGT 464). Oral debate rubric is used. Final Panel Presentation (SMGT 464). Oral presentation rubric used. Final Negotiation Scenario (SMGT 465). Negotiation scoring rubric is used. Final Negotiation Agreement Scenario (SMGT 465). Negotiation scoring rubric is used. Final Actual Mediation Scenario (SMGT 365). Mediation specific rubric used, as well as participant evaluations. All assessments directly related to course materials (text, required reading, handouts, videos, discussion questions, etc.) and information covered in class. Topics for assessments (SMGT 365, 464, 465) all sports industry related and addressed during the course. Test 1 SMGT 464 and SMGT 365/ Weekly Discussion Questions Questions are taken directly from the course materials. A grading key is used to ensure the grading is consistent and points appropriately allocated
results_conclusions: SMGT 365, 464, 465 â€“ Since these are very practical skills being taught students are mostly required to be in class and engaged in the activities in order to gain the most benefit. In general students did well in the class. However, there were some issues around students having a clear understanding of whether they were properly executing certain skills. Individual feedback in-the-moment was not always sufficient and not always absorbed by the other students through observation. Exercises during classes, though helpful, should also be routinely scored (365, 465) for students to gain the most benefit. Although exercises where routinely scored (464) difficult to manage the active debates and provide in-the-moment feedback on the topics to the students. Perhaps having a TA who can assist with conducting debates (timing responses, reading questions, will allow more opportunity for instructor feedback, coaching, (writing comments, scoring, etc.). Alternatively, remove timing and simply ask questions and/or limit number of questions or have students manage timing.
improvement_plan: Add additional scored assessments: Scored Negotiation 1 (SMGT 465) Scored Negotiation 2 (SMGT 465) Scored Mediation 1 (SMGT 365) Scored Mediation 2 (SMGT 365) Final Team Legal Research Paper (SMGT 464) Each of the above classes have only been taught once. These additional assessments will be implemented during this next school year (2018-2019) or the second time these classes will be taught. At the end of this second class for each of these the impact will be assessed. Total timeline by end of Spring 2019. Adjust SMGT 464 to remove timed questions, identify other means of students exercising legal research, legal reasoning, legal analysis on sport management related topics and current events. Adjust SMGT 465 to allow students to collaborate on final negotiation scenario.

department: Classical Studies (program housed in department of MCLC
plo: Communicate both orally and in writing the historical significance of ancient Greek and Roman culture and their continuing influence on the modern world.
methods: I based my direct assessment of this outcome on the written work on guided reading assignments completed and submitted by all the students in my course CLAS 124 Classical Antiquity in Children's Literature, which I taught in fall 2022.  These assignments were the basis for class discussions, and in my comments below I am accounting for what I recall of those as well as the content of the written work I have just reviewed. I chose to base this assessment on this material because the overarching goal of the course is that students achieve the outcome described above in respects specific to the course.  As indicated in its catalog description, students who enroll in the course study children's literature from the Victorian period up to the present day in which models from classical antiquity and/or the idea of classical antiquity itself are prominent, in an effort to understand the meanings "classical antiquity" held and holds for their authors and readers, and the agendas they served and serve. As usual, I elected not to use a rubric.  My reasons are the same as in previous years.  My personal experience of using rubrics is that when I do so, I am less attentive to subtleties and nuances in the word that I read and assess.
results_conclusions: On the whole, I was pleased to be reminded, on re-reading this work, that all of the students had substantially understood what they had read about the use of classical models and themes in authors such as Hawthorne, Kingsley, Beatrix Potter, J. M. Barrie, Frances Hodgson Burnett, Rosemary Sutcliff, the authors of the AstĂ©rix series, and others, and acquired an awareness of the prevalence of those models and themes that they had not had before.  One student actually commented several times that although she had read several of the books before, and also seen film versions of some of them, she was astonished by how much she had previously "missed," in the sense that she had not been aware previously of the classical references.  This was gratifying for me to read, of course.  The students, as I had anticipated and hoped, also frequently commented in class on such contemporary authors as Rick Riordan (and, to a lesser extent, J. K. Rowling) and compared their uses of classical material with those they were encountering in the assigned texts. They were also, to a varying degree, able to respond appropriately to the secondary texts I assigned for the course.  In this connection, all the students demonstrated that they had acquired a basic understanding of Romantic ideas about childhood and how those had shaped some of the authors' use of material from Greek religion and myth.  They also all seemed to understand how Hawthorne and Kingsley's different views about childhood and about the trajectory of human history were reflected in their retellings of Greek myth for child audiences.  They also all noted the inherently ambiguous nature of Aesop's fables, and how this had enabled their use, over and over again, to serve the ideology of quite different political and religious groups.  They wrote knowledgeably about the various contexts in which the fables had been used repeatedly both in the service of teaching reading and in moral education.  When we read and discussed novels for children and young adults set in ancient Rome and in Roman Britain, they were quick to identify and critique examples of cultural stereotyping.  With respect to Caroline Lawrence's Roman Mysteries series, specifically, they were able to appreciate the author's efforts to do justice to the cultural diversity of imperial Rome, but also quick to point outâ€”much more astutely than any of the assigned secondary literature, I might addâ€”the degree to which this resulted in stereotyped and flat characterization of the child protagonists. That said, some students demonstrated a much better grasp than others of the different approaches to the Romantic "child" reflected in the use of classical material by different Victorian and Edwardian writers.  Similarly, some students came away with a far more sophisticated understanding of how not only historical context, but also changing theories and practices in classical archaeology can explain the different descriptions of cultural difference and interaction in novels about Rome and Roman Britain published at different "moments" in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.   And while all the students demonstrated a good general understanding cultural stereotyping in the AstĂ©rix series, and of how the plots and characterization were likely shaped and influenced by twentieth century European history and educational traditions, some students were able to contribute a great deal more of their own that was insightful about the extent to which the series has or has not caught on in other parts of the world, such as the United States and Asian countries, and the possible reasons. These differences seemed to a large extent to correspond not only to the students' previous preparationâ€”whether or not they had taken other courses in classics or archaeologyâ€”but also to their ability to tackle secondary literature.  Some of them obviously found it much harder than others to make sense of an academic article, and this showed when they proved unable to engage in meaningful discussion of the concepts I have just described.
improvement_plan: Based on my last comment above, I plan to devote more class time, next time I teach this course, to working through the secondary material I assign, paragraph by paragraph if necessary, in the classroom, to make sure that students are all in a position to make the best possible use of it.  This will in and of itself be one way to help bridge the gap between students who are more or less well prepared for the class based on their previous experience.  I will also try to identify other, more basic, readings that will help accomplish that goal. I also plan to suggest to my colleagues that we aim to offer more courses that, like this one, focus on classical reception.  I believe that this is called for if we are serious, as we surely should be, about including this learning outcome, along with PLO 4, on our list.  I say this becauseâ€”as will be clear from my report this year on PLO 4â€”it seems very difficult to do justice to these learning outcomes in courses that do not make them their primary focus.  At least, that is my own experience.  I find that there is simply not sufficient time in a course that is primarily focused on the ancient texts and concepts read in their original contexts to help students not only be aware of the later and continuing influence of classical ideas, but also examine the phenomena in appropriate depth.  But I will also consult my colleagues about whether their experience is the same as mine. I will be sure to raise these questions and share these recommendations with my colleagues when we have our next round of discussion about course offerings, and will report on where we stand on the matters a year from now, in May or June 2024.  In the meantime, I will share this report with them and invite them to comment on this point along with the rest of its content.

plo: provide students with the ability to use intellectual tools to conduct scholarship associated with African and African American Studies.
methods: Direct observation of the outcome of the studentâ€™s work in the form of their dissertations.  Each student must move through a series of courses and experiences, and their dissertations must demonstrate an engagement with African and African American Studies scholarly methods, approaches, and modes of analysis.
results_conclusions: All dissertations addressed the field in substantive ways. Moreover, several of them are in African and African American Studies related or based academic posts.
improvement_plan: The current evaluative process is timely and builds upon other systems of direct observation. I do not anticipate any changes to the method or schedule of evaluation. The certificate will continue to undergo evaluation as planned.

department: Chemistry, Physics and Astronomy/Chemical Physics BS
plo: Demonstrate a solid foundation of knowledge in chemistry as applicable to chemical physics.
methods: Course grades for CHEM 211 and (CHEM 215 or CHEM 365) Course grades are based on extensive problem-solving homework and final exams so they are more indicative of overall performance than exams or individual assignments.
results_conclusions: Both students demonstrated mastery with average grades above 3.5 for the two courses.

plo: Demonstrate a solid foundation of knowledge in chemistry as applicable to chemical physics.
methods: Course grades for CHEM 211 and (CHEM 215 or CHEM 365) Course grades are based on extensive problem-solving homework and final exams so they are more indicative of overall performance than exams or individual assignments.
results_conclusions: Organic Chemistry: 1 student demonstrated proficiency with a grade of C+; two students demonstrated mastery with grades above B. Organic Chemistry lab: All three students demonstrated mastery with grades of A. The standard was easily met for this cohort.
improvement_plan: None N/A N/A

plo: Complete a dissertation proposal; Communicate research effectively by writing clearly, concisely, and cogently.
methods: In the fourth year the students finalize their dissertation committee, and submit a dissertation proposal. The dissertation proposal consists of a preliminary outline or draft of all dissertation chapters. The guidelines and assessment criterion are: Include the title of the dissertation and component chapters; identify dissertation committee members. For each chapter, define and motivate research question(s), summarize the related literature, and explain the contribution. If the topic of a chapter is methodological, specify the model and assumptions, and provide preliminary results. If the topic of a chapter is empirical, specify data source(s), explain the empirical strategy, and provide preliminary results. A proposed chapter on empirical topics must be linked to specific data source(s). Access to the data should be secured and there should be exploratory analysis of the data. Otherwise, the proposal must specify a feasible timeline for data acquisition. Suggested length of the dissertation proposal: 15-20 pages for the main chapter (a.k.a. the job market paper), and 5-10 pages for the other chapters.
results_conclusions: All fourth year students have passed this requirement.
improvement_plan: We are satisfied with these results and do not plan to make any changes.

plo: Conduct a focused review of the literature and develop a research design to carry out independent research; develop a research agenda for supervised work toward dissertation as well as independent research
methods: Direct assessment: Each second-year student is enrolled in Econ 598 (Advanced Topics and Research Methods) or Econ 800 (Graduate Research) under the supervision of a second year mentor. The goal is to guide second-year Ph.D. students to conduct quantitative research. After aÂ critical review ofÂ existingÂ economic models, statistical analysis of data and economic evaluations, students develop their own research agenda.
results_conclusions: All current second year Ph.D. students satisfy this requirement.
improvement_plan: We are satisfied with these results and do not plan to make any changes.

plo: Explain the historical trajectory of at least two of these Ancient Mediterranean Civilizations: Graeco-Roman, Islamic, Jewish, Christian.
methods: I reviewed two research papers he completed in two classes: For Greco-Roman, I examined his paper on the late Roman author Ammianus Marcellinus. I also discussed the paper with him during its preparation and afterwards. The paper was evaluated for depth of research, analysis of ancient sources, development of modern scholarly interpretations, construction of argument based on that analysis, preparation of notes and bibliography, and final version of the paper. In all of these areas he did first rate work, receiving an A in the course and on the paper. My evaluation was based on more than 30 years of experience teaching and research. The student compares most favorably with other students I have taught during this period. For Christian civilization, I followed the same procedure and rubrics for a course on Medieval Spain taught by another professor. I reached the same conclusions about his work.
results_conclusions: The student has learned important skills well, especially in doing research in ancient sources, constructing arguments, and in clear expository writing. In his exit interview with me he said that learning these things has been a great source of accomplishment to him. He values what he has learned.

department: Accounting PhD Program, Jones Graduate School of Business
plo: Understand advanced accounting concepts, theories, principles, and quantitative methods including strategic role of accounting in business organizations and society.
methods: Students in the first two years of the program take courses in which they are evaluated by their professors on the traditional grade based system. After completing two years of coursework students take a comprehensive exam administered by the Accounting PhD Program director in conjunction with the Accounting Faculty.  Students are provided a series of questions over a 1 to 1.5 day period that they must answer in writing. The exam is closed book and notes thus the students must have a strong command of the accounting literature along with the ability to demonstrate knowledge of research design techniques. Once the question and answer portion is complete the students are given an accounting research paper to review. They must critically evaluate the research question, design, methods, contribution, and conclusions drawn from the study. Two faculty members are assigned to grade each question independently.  The Accounting PhD Program director collects the grades and determines whether a student passed or failed in conjunction with the members grading each portion. Once the final determination is made the students are notified.  In the case of a failing grade, the student is allotted a second opportunity during the third year to pass the qualifying exam, which may be oral or in writing at the discretion of the Accounting faculty members.  If the student does not pass on this second attempt the student is dismissed from the program.
results_conclusions: We have three students that will be taking the exam on June 24-26, 2020.
improvement_plan: There are no changes necessary to our program at this point in time.

plo: Execute and present original research in their discipline.
methods: For the doctoral dissertation and defense, PhD students are required to execute and present original research in their discipline.  We had seven PhD students who graduated in May of 2016.  They all executed and presented original research in their disciplines and all passed their dissertation defenses as determined by their dissertation committee chairs or co-chairs and the faculty on their dissertation committees.  Additionally, I emailed each of the chairs or co-chairs of each of these seven PhD students and asked them if this learning objective/outcome was accomplished by the studentsâ€™ doctoral dissertations and defenses.  All chairs and co-chairs indicated that the objective/outcome was accomplished by the doctoral dissertations and defenses.
results_conclusions: This learning objective/outcome is being accomplished by PhD students through the doctoral dissertation and defense.
improvement_plan: Based on these results, no changes will be made.
